metadata.summary: Systematic criteria to evaluate market segment viability: market size (TAM/SAM/SOM), competition positioning, audience accessibility, monetization potential, and personal fit.

content.title: Niche Evaluation
content.summary: After generating ideas, solopreneurs struggle to evaluate which niche is worth pursuing. Niche evaluation is the process of analyzing a market segment to determine if it's viable for your business. A good niche is specific enough to dominate but large enough to sustain revenue.

why.p: Choosing based on gut feeling without data leads to picking niches too broad (competing with giants) or too small (no revenue potential). Ignoring competition until it's too late is a common mistake. A systematic scoring framework prevents bias and converts subjective decisions into comparable priority lists.

list.item: Choosing among 3-10 candidate niches before committing to a product direction.
Solopreneur narrowing-down stage: ideas list to top 3 to validate.
Re-evaluating an existing niche after market signals shift (new entrants, regulation, AI commoditization).
Pitching investors / stakeholders and needing a defensible scoring rubric.
After committing — switch to problem-validation, then feature-discovery.
For very small experiments (one-week tests) where decision overhead exceeds upside.
When you have only one niche option — score it but don't pretend the comparison is meaningful.
For commodity markets where the niche is irrelevant (execution speed wins).

rule.statement: Define the niche precisely using this formula: [Product Type] for [Specific Audience] who [Specific Problem].
rule.rationale: Vague definitions prevent effective market research and evaluation. "Project management software" is unsearchable; "task tracking for freelance designers who struggle with client deadlines" is specific enough to research and distinguish from competitors.

example.code: Good: "Task tracking for freelance designers who struggle with client deadlines" Bad: "Project management software"

rule.statement: Calculate potential using TAM (Total Addressable Market), SAM (Serviceable Addressable Market), and SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market). Targets: TAM ≥$1B, SAM ≥$100M, SOM ≥$1M.
rule.rationale: These metrics provide a reality-check on market scale. A niche with TAM <$50M is unlikely to sustain a venture; one with SOM <$1M won't even cover living expenses for a solopreneur.

list.item: TAM: Industry reports, Statista, Gartner, LinkedIn learning data.
SAM: Your geography/segment slices the TAM.
SOM: Realistic capture (often 1-5% of SAM in year 3).

rule.statement: Quick validation using Google Trends (is interest growing?), keyword volume (are people searching?), and community size (are people talking?).
rule.rationale: Declining interest curves and low search volume signal a shrinking niche; community size (Reddit/Discord) indicates demand density and researchability.

rule.statement: Map competitors on a 2x2 matrix: Quality vs. Number of Players. Target position: High quality, few competitors.
rule.rationale: Quadrants reveal strategy: Opportunity Zone (high quality, low competition) is ideal; Premium segment (high quality, many) means proven market but crowded; Race to Bottom (low quality, many) requires speed; Crowded Market (low quality, high quantity) is red flag.

list.item: Always map at least 5 competitors.
If fewer found, flag "low competition signal" with reasoning (emerging category vs. dead market).
Include non-obvious competitors: DIY solutions (spreadsheets, Notion templates), adjacent tools, "do nothing" option.

rule.statement: Score audience accessibility 1-5 across: organic reach (Reddit/Twitter/etc), dedicated communities (Discord/Slack), media presence (podcasts/blogs), paid ads availability, and spending power.
rule.rationale: Target average score >3.5. Low accessibility (score <2) means high customer acquisition cost and makes validation interviews hard. If you can't reach the audience affordably, the niche is inefficient regardless of size.

example.code:
High accessibility: - Subreddit with 50k+ members posting daily - 10+ podcasts covering the niche - Paid ads available on G2 / industry forums - Community willing to pay ($50+/month)
Low accessibility: - Scattered Twitter mentions only - No aggregated community spaces - No relevant podcast coverage - Gatekept professional group (NDA restrictions)

rule.statement: Evaluate revenue models (SaaS, course, templates, consulting, ads) and price points. Question: Can you charge $50+/month or $200+ one-time?
rule.rationale: Willingness to pay is the binding constraint. A large niche that pays $5/month cannot sustain a solopreneur; a small niche paying $500/mo is more viable. Avoid niches where the cost-to-serve > revenue.

list.item: SaaS: $X/mo, recurring.
Course: $X once, no recurring.
Templates: $X once, no recurring.
Consulting: $X/hr, varies.
Ads: CPM model, yes recurring.

rule.statement: Rate honestly on a 1-5 scale: audience understanding, relevant skills, daily excitement, content-creation ability, network connections. If average <3, reconsider even if market is great.
rule.rationale: Passion matters but won't compensate for bad market. Conversely, a good market with low personal fit means you'll burn out or produce mediocre work. This dimension must come from a self-interview, not auto-scored by agents.

rule.statement: Compute final score: (Market × 0.25) + (Competition × 0.20) + (Accessibility × 0.15) + (Monetization × 0.20) + (Personal Fit × 0.20). Thresholds: >4.0 Excellent, 3.5-4.0 Good, 3.0-3.5 Risky, <3.0 Reconsider.
rule.rationale: The weights reflect priority: market size dominates, personal fit and monetization are co-equal gates, competition and accessibility are supporting factors. These proportions are heuristic, not calibrated; adjust for your risk tolerance.

antipattern.reason: Add "for [specific audience]" qualifier; if it matches more than 5% of the internet, it's not a niche.
Always map at least 5 competitors; "no competition" usually means dead market, not blue ocean.
Passion matters but won't compensate for bad market; a bad niche with high personal fit is still a bad niche.
Even small niches should be quantified; a niche with <$1M SOM can't support a business.
Validation requires actual conversations; scoring alone is hypothesis-spinning.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Score each candidate niche through five subagent passes (market, competition, accessibility, monetization, personal fit). Each pass returns JSON with score plus 3 evidence citations. A separate aggregator agent computes the weighted total and ranks niches; the human picks based on ranking plus qualitative gut. Run the comparison sheet side-by-side, never one niche at a time. Personal fit must come from a self-interview, not auto-scored by agents.

list.item: Score at least 3 niches in parallel; absolute scores mean little without comparison.
Lock weights before scoring; tweaking weights to favor a preferred niche is the most common bias.
Require evidence citations for any score ≥4 — this is the agent's most-fabricated zone.
Personal fit must come from a self-interview, not an auto-score; LLMs flatter the user.
Re-score niches every quarter; market entries / regulation can flip the ranking.
Use Reddit/Discord post frequency as a leading indicator — declining communities = declining niche.
LLMs hallucinate TAM numbers when the niche is narrow (e.g., "AI tools for solo notaries"). Require source URLs or "low confidence".
"Few competitors" can mean blue ocean OR no demand. Force the agent to distinguish via search-volume plus adjacent-product data.
Agents over-weight high-quality SaaS competitors and miss spreadsheet/Notion-template alternatives — explicitly include "current workarounds" in the competition prompt.
Personal fit auto-scoring leaks the user's enthusiasm; surface it as a question, never compute it.
When asked to compare niches, agents pick the one mentioned first; randomize order.
Personal-fit weight (20%) is the most-faked dimension; founders inflate it for ideas they like.
TAM/SAM/SOM numbers from analyst reports are commonly off by an order of magnitude; treat as ranges.
Competition matrix becomes static the day you build it; refresh quarterly.
Accessibility scoring (Reddit/Twitter/podcasts) overrates discoverability for B2B niches where decision-makers don't post publicly.
A high score doesn't survive contact with one bad interview; this method gates exploration, not decisions.

template.purpose: Complete evaluation scorecard template with all 5 factors, scoring matrix, and final decision.
